speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two. This is January 24th, 1996. Our survivor is Rabbi Nathan Lipschitz. Could you tell us about your brothers and sisters, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were six children, four boys and two girls. My brother, the oldest one, was Lezer-- Elezer was his name. He was born in 1913. Sit and learn in beth midrash. And but there was an unfortunate thing happened with him, that he was taken to the army. And this was a considered a tragedy. Normally a, a family of a rabbi, son of a rabbi, did all kinds of things, but very seldom went to the army. In this case, it did not work out, and he was transferred to Lvov, to Polish army. And the Polish army for a Jewish boy, especially a religious boy, was really miserable. Now some of the Polish people asked, well, after all, you live in this country. You should be a patriot. Yeah. This is very well said, but we know how they behaved, how the Polacks were, were always cursing and doing do all kinds of dirty tricks to the Jewish boys. And besides, [? the ?] eating kosher and, of course, you know, observing Shabbos. So it was one of the big, big tragedies.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he when he was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 21 years was where you stayed for a commission, and they evaluate you, your health and everything, if you were good enough for the army. And he was 21 years old, and they called him in-- about less than a year later, he went to the army. It was a tragedy. And then a year-- a boy more or less his age, maybe a year older, went for us to Lvov to stay with him and bring him every day kosher foods, which was quite an expense. And then meanwhile, we were working somehow to be able to get him out of there. Now that, that fellow got-- somebody introduced him to a girl while he was there, and he got engaged and he couldn't stay there no more. So I went and stood with my brother a few weeks, and to bring him every day to the-- to the army base. What is it called-- to bring him kosher food. And after a short time-- altogether he wasn't more than a little over three months. Between three and four months, he came home. But it was-- the whole family-- it was a big tragedy. And then he came home and back to the normal routine of a little town, rabbi's sons. Whatever there was, the doings. There wasn't a question of-- of going to work, not even-- besides that there was nothing to work for. There was no jobs around. But even other boys, there was no work what to do. And that was my-- he got married. This is the one that got married before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did he marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: He married a girl, also a Lipschitz, from another little town, from, from the other side of Krakow. Sarah Lipschitz was her name. And her father was a rabbi, and the town was called Czarny Dunajec. Dunajec-- there was a river flowing there that was called Dunajec. And it was dark water, so it's called Czarny Dunajec, the black river. And the father was a rabbi there. His name was also [INAUDIBLE] Lipschitz, also related. And when we went back from that little town, we went into our zayde's place in Briegel, which is Brzesko in, in Polish. And we stood there for a while. And the last few days before the war, my father was there from the family and I was there. And my sister-- married sister-- I had a sister, Hinda, that was married. She was younger than my brother, but through some sort circumstances, she was married a little before him. And she was married with a fellow from the same town where the zayde lived. So she was still there, and I was there. My father said, that's too much going on. You know, in the air there was such a nervousness, talking so and so thick about the war. So he went back to our little town. He said, I still have young children. And he went back, and this is the last time I saw him. And I was there with my grandfather and my sister-- my sister with her husband. And after the war broke out, which was on a Friday, September the 1st, everybody got nervous, and everybody was thinking about running. So my-- finally, the next Tuesday they were saying that the Germans are not far, and the army is coming in. So we started up to walk. We wanted to buy horse and buggies. We couldn't buy anything anymore. And we started walking, and everybody thought it's going to be what they used to call in-- in a German word in Yiddish, [YIDDISH], [? they called it ?] [YIDDISH]. It means moving [INAUDIBLE] trains. [INAUDIBLE] It was so different between-- so we walked and walked until about 30 people went with the zayde because he was that spiritual leader, the rebbe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to that point, I'd still like to hear about your brothers and sisters, the rest of the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. And then we'll catch up to that. All right. Now as I mentioned just now, my sister was the second. Her name was Hinda.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her husband's name was Moishe Kapler from that same town, Briegel. And the father was also a rabbi, a great scholar. His name was [INAUDIBLE] Kapler. And she married only about a little over a year before the war. By the way, those, those picture with the Cossacks, when she got married, that's her when she got married. So this is the sister, the second. I was after, the third.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was the fourth child?

speaker: interviewee
text: The fourth child was another sister. Alte Miriam was her name-- young girl, stayed in the house. And then we had a brother, Chaim [? Myer, ?] who did also learn in the biggest town, in a yeshiva in Krakow. And then of course the holidays, and then he came home. And then the youngest one was Aron. He was the youngest. When the war broke out, I think he must have been about 15-- 14, 16, 8, and 20-- about 15. He is the one that was taken while all the town was still as our home, more or less. He was taken to a concentration camp which was Mielec because the Germans had given out a decree-- let's say, call it, I don't know-- that from every family has to give one person to go and work in that-- in that concentration camp that was in Mielec. And they were producing there airplanes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my son-- my brother was brought from our house that went there. He was there. He was fairly well off in comparison because the other people from town, because he was the rabbi's son, he was very lovable boy too. They took care of him so he shouldn't fall. But at the end, before the-- this wasn't too long before that the camp were dispersed. He got sick on typhoid, and he died. Could you tell us about a typical day for you in this town when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say they were not typical. Almost every was a little different. But the normal routine was getting up in the morning and go to beth midrash to learn, and then you go for breakfast. Then you go back to beth midrash, and then you're a little sleepy, so you-- you took-- you open the [NON-ENGLISH]. You put your hand-- the head on top of the hand to take a little snooze, and then you learned again. And after that, if you had a big [NON-ENGLISH], which means you-- so you kept on going until [NON-ENGLISH]. If not, you had a few fellows that also didn't feel like too much to-- to stick to the learning, so they talk. There wasn't anything else to do in that little town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe how long you were in school, what your schooling was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The schooling as the teenager-- first of all, I was 10 years old when-- my zayde had a gabbai who was like his personal servant and everything. So he asked my father, you complain that-- now I had, as far as learning, I had like it's an expression in-- in Hebrew, a-- a Talmudical expression [HEBREW]. Sometimes you're normal, sometimes you're not normal. My father said it to me. If I set out to learn, I could have learned without stop, practically 18, 20 hours that he begged me to stop, because he was afraid I-- [INAUDIBLE] And if I didn't feel like learning, he could chop me up and I wouldn't. So this was unfortunately, my, my [? stubborn ?] made him very unhappy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but-- but at 10 years old, that one suggested to my father, because he told him, you know, complained. He says, why don't you send him to yeshiva? So he sent me to Krakow. I was only 10 years old. To me it was paradise [SIGHS] because he didn't bother me so much. I was out of his [? style ?].

speaker: interviewee
text: This was 1928. And my father went in with my grandfather and an uncle to Germany, to Berlin, because my, my, my father's youngest brother had polio on the left side-- the leg, with a hand, [INAUDIBLE]. So somehow it came out rumors, and some people said they're sure that in Berlin there is some professor who can cure that. My grandfather, and aunt, my father, and that uncle went to Berlin. Stood there several weeks and the doctor, the professor wasn't there. I mean, it was a farce, the whole thing. This could not be cured. Then they talk about operation. They couldn't make it over. We know now until-- that there was nothing to be done. But they were there for a while, and then I was in Krakow. I so happy that my father sent, to a big boy there, money for me. [LAUGHS] So I was in heaven. But then I was only about a year because I was still very young. I studied in the yeshiva. And then after I got bar mitzvah I came back to Krakow. And I was quite a few years off and on in the yeshivas there in Krakow. But the major learning was done in beth midrash. An older boy who knew good how to learn, learned with one or two or three boys. And this was the style of learning mostly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any particular friends, and their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had friends, but you mean from my little town? I had a very close friend, was named Sertoiya, Yeshia Sertoiya, Meyer Schlesinger, Yankel Barrat. This was-- they weren't always in the same age, but with the two of them, we learned together. We were very close. There were quite a few friends. I mean, everybody had some friends. As a boy, you had some friends, some very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you spend your free time if you weren't in beth midrash?

speaker: interviewee
text: There wasn't any thing to spend it. There was nothing. We went-- there was nothing to do, just talk between us. There was nothing to do. When I'm thinking now about I wonder how we all stood sane. I say we-- not those that knew how to learn. And there was a beth midrash full of [INAUDIBLE]. You never get lonesome, really, if you don't have what to do. But people that didn't, I don't know how they didn't go off their rocker. I mean, there was nothing to be done in that little town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe Shabbos in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like every Jewish home, a Shabbos. Our father came home. We all came home from shul. And said [NON-ENGLISH]. But we always had somebody-- you know, it's something. You asked me something. There was between those travelers, poor people, some of them were even sick, and-- I-- I talk about it because sometimes we were tortured. They were smelly. They were smelly from-- from having some sickness, open wounds. Or smelly from [INAUDIBLE]. Some kids wanted to laugh. If we would laugh, our father would have killed us. And we had to  sit through the whole meal. And this went down very often, very often. But no, when the Shabbos. Father had shul. [NON-ENGLISH] [LAUGHS] I don't know if I should say that. But you asked me already, so sort of a intimate question already.

speaker: interviewee
text: But [LAUGHS] I remember once, that I [INAUDIBLE]-- if I was bent down, my father said, why do you have the whole cup in your-- your-- your food. [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHS] If I stood straight, what are you sitting like? He complained I'm sitting too straight, then it's almost [NON-ENGLISH] So this-- this went together with a-- with a good feeling of Shabbos too, a Shabbos meal, as well always. Shabbos meal, there was always very, very happy occasions and feelings. It was spiritually, that we didn't realize even then that it was spiritually. But it was spiritual.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was always plenty good food, Shabbos food and [NON-ENGLISH] in our house. Other people, we, we-- my mother used to send me or my sister to about six to 10 people with a half a chicken, or with a challah, or two challahs, that really didn't have Shabbos. Didn't have-- and other people did that too. But I, I go from our house. Half a chicken, in some case [INAUDIBLE] because a single fellow, was a quarter of a chicken, a half a chicken and-- and some challahs.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Pesach like in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pesach was, everybody was in such a spirit that it's hard to explain spiritual things, you know, the spiritual feeling because first of all, the weeks before, everybody was busy. All this food were out on tables to get some air. In case of chametz, you should throw away, the wind should blow it away. Almost everybody was painting. And everybody was busy before Pesach. And then a few days before Pesach, first of all, the matzahs baker-- there was only one bakery. And most of the poor people were working there, especially the women and the elder girls made a few dollars then. It was for them, the-- once a year, the-- the-- the so called earning time. And there was-- the-- the whole spirit was there with them packing the matzahs, taking home the matzahs was-- This was the thing of the-- of the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then [INAUDIBLE] everybody went to, to to near the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] there was a-- a bath house. Right near they was doing the kashruting the pots and pans before Pesach. The kids had a good time and running around. This was their excitement, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then when we ate of [? Pesach, ?] breaking the matzahs was a very, very spiritual thing. Almost the whole town was there. It wasn't this one bakery, and the same, the [INAUDIBLE] for the baking. This was all spiritual. Everybody was lifted. Everybody was a new person. This, you cannot describe those in words, the feeling. But it was like a new-- you came into a new atmosphere completely, especially Pesach. The kids were wearing those-- those new clothes. And they looked at those shoes, and they went like this, to wipe off in case a little spot went because it was once a year that put the shoes. And then the little suit [INAUDIBLE] they had on, they was-- they were shining. That was-- in poverty, that was things that-- that everybody almost felt, now everybody's rich almost. Especially the kids felt so different. It was something that you can-- could touch it with your hand if you wanted to observe it, if your eyes caught it. If you weren't always used to it or you want to observe it, you had that feeling, that great feeling, that even the poor felt good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before 1939, what were you doing? What was-- what was your plan for the future or your dream?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no any plan for the future. You know, four or five-- up to about four or five years before the war, you came in-- especially a-- a winter night-- you came into beth midrash. That was the place. That was the club. That was everything, you know-- the theater, the club, the movie house, I mean, talking in today's terms. There were a lot of [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH]-- that means the single fellows-- sitting and learning, sitting and learning with life. Slowly, slowly they stopped. And this was all because the poverty got worse. There was no hope. People were in those little towns who were considered [NON-ENGLISH] middle-class or lower, didn't hardly have anything to eat or harder. That knocked the people around spiritually, even physically. They weren't interested in nothing. They went around looking. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a few left that still kept. But I did ask, I remember one, a very funny little episode. A young man-- an older young single fellow came into the beth midrash, and said to a kid, you know, go buy me some fruit. You know, buy me some pears. He gave him $0.10. He says which kind? He says any kind that gets the cheapest. It should be enough to eat. He was so hungry. But this, I could not forget that this-- I was a kid. There is more. Then I waited and he came in. And he was eating that, you could've sworn that he was hungry so badly, didn't have what to eat for quite awhile. This was--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, when we talk about those things, I don't know if I should say that. Also mention that-- mention that there isn't a day, even during the day, but especially at night going to bed, that I shouldn't go through this whole thing. What bothers me the most-- now we're believers and we believe in God and everything. [INAUDIBLE] But the thing that bothers me the most, I would say is when you know a little bit about history, starting from there was a first temple and then a second temple. The Jewish nation was-- had a home, a country, a temple, that the whole life was revolving around it, normal life. You could say on top a beautiful life, all the time of the second temple.

speaker: interviewee
text: So if we were sinning and it was destroyed, it was from people who had stature, who had wealth, who had nachas. And the same thing with the second. When we talk about Spain, people were also very well off, very recognized in the country culturally, and, and monetary. This here-- [INAUDIBLE] so you understand the punishment, what we believe it's all a punishment. Why, what, we're, we're not supposed to ask.

speaker: interviewee
text: But from this year, where there were so much troubles in the Jewish community as a whole that anybody that looked into it couldn't hardly believe what was going on in the normal life, with discriminations, with taking away [NON-ENGLISH] taking away livelihoods from Jewish people so often. There were thousands of families, or maybe tens of [? thousands, ?] in the villages that had some sort of livelihood. They were driven out. They were burned out and driven out in a normal time. And from this to get such a punishment, this is so hard to comprehend that you get the goosepimps again and again and again. And I don't know what to say about this-- this, when I talk about or think about it. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Should I answer it? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There's the-- there's the telephone.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship between the Jews and the non-Jews in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no real relationship. Here and there, maybe a priest-- usually. It started a priest. He was steaming up the people sometimes. They were here and there, make some. But normally, there was a normal, normal relationship. They came to buy and they were friends, or friendly. There was no-- only some of them carried on with-- with the steam up the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do-- do you remember from the rise of the Nazis at that time? Do you remember any incidents or anything that you knew happening about the Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean when they-- when they occupied Poland?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before that. Did you hear about them before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sure. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that they were up to destroy-- to destroy the Jews. They were talking, writing. Listen, we knew all what the was saying. But who believed it? Some politician is talking to get the people to listen to him. Who believe it? There was no anybody that took it very seriously.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was some-- there were some politicians who were in, in the head of communities in the big cities, who were also, some were in the-- Jewish people who were in the [NON-ENGLISH] or Senate, you know, which was part of the government that did warn here and there. But then you was-- normal life for hundreds of years in that community with the families. Where you gonna go? You couldn't get a visa to go any other part of the world. So everybody says, somebody should let [INAUDIBLE] There were a few people-- a few. I mean, it's relative, the saying, [INAUDIBLE] probably thousands or ten of thousands, maybe hundreds of thousands that did, and would have, were bitter enough to go to emigrate. But there was hardly anybody, any country wanted to let us in. Even before the war, if you waited for a visa to America, you waited for years until your, your number will show up. So there wasn't much that you could do. Did you know anybody who did leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. There were a few people that left, very few. Very few people that left. They-- the people that came back to the little town to visit, they were in foreign countries, like in Germany or America. They-- Germany, yes, you could right after the war go. But they were immigrating before all the restrictions. So they came away as guests to visit their little town. They work themselves up a bit. But people that emigrated from my time to a foreign country, I hardly remember. I would have to think very, very hard to remind myself of any.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned September 1st, 1939. What do you remember about that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was bewildered. I mean, nervous, nobody knew what to do. There was nobody to talk to. And people were running back and forth. [INAUDIBLE] Yeah, this-- I was in my grandfather's home. This, I remember. There was [NON-ENGLISH], you know, a follower from-- from Nowe Sacz, from another town. He was a [NON-ENGLISH] And because we were there, he wanted to be near the rebbe. So he was there for the Shabbos. He came for that Shabbos. And that Friday morning, when everybody started to holler war broke out, he took all kinds of rags, towels, [INAUDIBLE] and put near the doors, being afraid of gas. We were afraid there will be gas. And this was a panic in that house. I don't know-- [INAUDIBLE] yes, the first thing we were afraid was gas. And this went on for a few days. And as I said, on this, the next Tuesday, we started to emigrate, to walk. Now this, I don't know how to, to tell you this or if it's interesting to you. Now my-- as I said, my grandfather was a Hasidic rabbi. There was always people after him. You know, the rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he decided we are going off, we're starting to [NON-ENGLISH], he came out in-- in the front of the house. And there were women over there who came from [INAUDIBLE] from some, they came over here. They ran away from the Germans. And local people crying [NON-ENGLISH], why do you leave us? [INAUDIBLE] And he was, why this war, why? And he stood there, and then he says to me-- he was tall. He was-- now [INAUDIBLE] maybe 6'4'. And I am much shorter than that. He says to me, do you have another [NON-ENGLISH], another? I says, yes. Do you have another hat? I says, yes. He says take, take it with you, take the other one because I had a principle-- I don't know if you knew it, but I had a principle, as little as possible. If there will be a world we're going to the normal places. We'll get what we need, what we have-- not to schlep packages. So he says take it along with you. So I said should I put one on top? He says, whatever you want, fine. So I took it along. Then he said he had some very, very, very important [NON-ENGLISH] tefillin. It was an inheritance. And that's a whole story with that. So he says to me, go up and take this along with you. But guard it with your life because this was so important to him. Guard it with your life. And when I came down with the clothing, and he looked. Saw there were a few men there. And he said to them. I said, rabbi, you know why I asked him to take these clothes along? I know on the way-- We're going to stop at this point--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and we'll give the answer.
